source: All-In Podcast — E125: SpaceX Starship launch, Fox News settlement, AI/Reddit
summary: ~70-80% of late-stage private tech companies (Series C+) are worth less than their preference stack — a reckoning has not yet begun
summary_extended: Baker (and Chamath, whose views are being discussed) argues that 70-80% of late-stage private tech companies (Series C+) are worth less than their preference stack, meaning common equity is effectively worthless for the vast majority of venture-backed unicorns. The analytical framework cited is that roughly 70% of tech companies that went public in the last three years trade at enterprise values below the cumulative cash they have raised and burned, and Chamath extrapolates this dynamic to private markets where marks have not yet been written down. Tiger Global's ~20% markdown is cited as a concrete but insufficient example of the reckoning to come, and Chamath personally validated the thesis by selling a portfolio company at one-third of its marked value with private equity buyers confirming the lower valuation. The investment implication is that a broad private market valuation reset is imminent but has barely begun, suggesting continued pressure on late-stage VC funds, crossover investors, and any public market vehicles with exposure to these assets — though no specific publicly traded equity is a clear direct subject or beneficiary of this thesis.
detail: Chamath cites that 70% of tech companies that went public in the last 3 years trade below the cash they've raised (Enterprise Value < cumulative retained earnings deficit). Extrapolating to private markets, 70-80% of Series C+ companies are likely worth less than their preference stack. Most marks have not been written down. Tiger Global's ~20% markdown is cited as insufficient. The reckoning is described as 'metastasized' but 'hasn't even started.'
confidence_evidence: Chamath presents a specific analytical framework (EV vs cumulative retained earnings), cites a concrete statistic (70% of recent IPOs), and says he personally sold a portfolio company at a third of its marked value with PE validation. Sacks corroborates with a pay-to-play example.
quote_fragment: 70 of publicly traded companies that went public in the last three years are trading below the cash
